topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. The headline, the byline—it was all wrong. My meticulously researched piece, the one I’d poured months into, was attributed to 'Elias Vance.' My jaw tightened so hard I thought my teeth might crack. I reread the correspondence three times, a slow, deliberate process designed to cool the rising heat in my chest. I felt a distinct, almost physical clenching in my gut. I immediately drafted a reply, a barrage of pointed questions demanding clarification, my fingers flying across the keyboard with unnecessary force. I wouldn't let this slide. I printed the original submission and the published version side-by-side, the contrast stark and infuriating. I knew, deep down, that this was just the beginning of a fight, and I intended to meet every single challenge head-on.

[story 2]

When the notification popped up, a cold, sharp sensation shot down my spine. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a profound sense of being undermined. I stared at the screen, my breathing shallow. A slow, almost imperceptible tremor ran through my hands as I clicked on the article. Seeing my own analysis presented under another person's moniker felt like a deliberate slight. I stood up, pacing the confines of my small office. Each step was heavy, weighted with a refusal to accept the injustice. I pulled out my phone, the screen illuminating my face as I started composing a response. My tone, even in the drafting stage, was clipped, precise, leaving no room for ambiguity. I needed to establish the facts, and I was going to make sure everyone understood that this was not something to be ignored or swept under the rug.

[story 3]

The confirmation arrived, and the world seemed to tilt slightly off its axis. My initial reaction was a deep, internal hardening. I didn't shout; I didn't lash out. Instead, I sat perfectly still, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic rhythm of my pulse. I looked at the published piece, and the realization settled in my core like a stone. A slow, simmering resentment began to build, a low thrumming energy beneath my skin. I didn't immediately reach for the phone to call the editor. Instead, I took a long, slow breath, forcing the air deep into my lungs. I began drafting a rebuttal, word by careful word, ensuring every sentence was constructed to convey unwavering certainty. I felt a fierce resolve taking root, an unyielding determination to correct this error, no matter the resistance I encountered along the way.
